hypothesis: Keeping `lowrank_two_stage_residual` fixed at the latest executed point `K=1` and reducing only `EXPANSION_FACTOR` from 12 to 8 will test whether this family can deliver a strictly cheaper ultra-low-K point with acceptable additional FVU.
summary: Use a parameter-only probe on `lowrank_two_stage_residual` at `K=1`, changing only `EXPANSION_FACTOR` to `8` while pinning `OPTIMIZER=signum`, `LR=1.6e-3`, and `AUXK_ALPHA=0.0`. This repairs the prior isolation failure by holding `K` at the last successful run and probing the remaining frontier dimension cleanly.
self_review: This is the cleanest recovery after the policy rejection: it changes exactly one experimental dimension relative to the last real run, preserves the current family, and probes whether the ultra-low-K endpoint can also become cheaper.
notes_to_memory: Round 10 recovers from the prior policy rejection by holding `K` at the latest successful point (`K=1`) and changing only `EXPANSION_FACTOR`.
When the controller enforces strict isolation against the latest executed run, EF sweeps should start from the most recent successful K rather than jumping back to an older anchor.
next_hypotheses: If `K=1, EF=8` stays competitive, continue the EF sweep at the same `K=1` to `EF=6` to find the cost-quality floor.
If `K=1, EF=8` degrades too much, keep `EF=12` as the ultra-low-K recipe and try an EF sweep later from the `K=2` or `K=4` lane with strict sequential isolation.
If `K=1, EF=8` is borderline but promising, test `EF=10` at the same `K=1` to refine the knee.
